metadata.title: Claude, Scripted
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one accent; callout ink #161511 on cream; code stays Onda dark glass
metadata.note: COLD OPEN LAW: B00 = ClaudeComposerAsk. SPARK-LINE LAW: inner beats pair the spark with one short serif line; typing is B00-only. OUTRO LAW: outro restates the title. Spine: B00 ASK · B01 THE SCRIPT (Onda code-block: one real script call) · B02 REWRITE TAX · B03 THE AXIS (specifies vs influences — the series names it here) · B04 THE BELT (one script, all brands) · B05 FORK HORROR (generate_audio.py exists twice — honest self-own) · B06 THE LAW (reuse before you write) · B07 VERDICT (artifact: script or prompt?) · B08 title-restate outro.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Work that must come out exact — two runs must agree — belongs in a script; work that may vary belongs in a prompt. Re-deriving deterministic work every run is the rewrite tax, paid in tokens and in drift. One belt of scripts serves every reel and brand, so there is only one place to debug. The law is reuse before you write: call an existing script, parameterize one that almost fits, write new code last.

props.artifactTitle: Scripts own exact, prompts own variable
artifactLines: Work that must come out exact — two runs must agree — belongs in a script; work that may vary belongs in a prompt.
Re-deriving deterministic work every run is the rewrite tax, paid in tokens and in drift.
One belt of scripts serves every reel and brand, so there is only one place to debug.
The law is reuse before you write: call an existing script, parameterize one that almost fits, write new code last.

beats.narration_text: The decision rule — scripts own the exact, prompts own the variable — is the line I want to apply to my own pipeline. I have a step that measures mp3s and writes a timing file. Using your 'ceiling of duration plus 0.4, times 30' example: (1) show me why that belongs in a script rather than a prompt, (2) spell out what the rewrite tax would cost me in tokens and drift if I re-derived it each run, and (3) give me the test for deciding whether any new step is exact or variable.

props.command: The decision rule — scripts own the exact, prompts own the variable — is the line I want to apply to my own pipeline. I have a step that measures mp3s and writes a timing file. Using your 'ceiling of duration plus 0.4, times 30' example: (1) show me why that belongs in a script rather than a prompt, (2) spell out what the rewrite tax would cost me in tokens and drift if I re-derived it each run, and (3) give me the test for deciding whether any new step is exact or variable.

beats.narration_text: One call. Same result every time, regardless of reel, brand, or model. It measures the mp3s, calculates the frames, writes the timing file. Same answer this run as last run. That consistency is the feature.

props.title: the belt — one call
props.code:
$ python3 runtime/scripts/generate_audio.py \
    youtube/claude-scripted/
# measures mp3s, calculates frames
# writes timing json, updates beat_sheet
# same script · every reel · every brand

beats.narration_text: The alternative is asking Claude to re-derive it every run. Same ffmpeg flags, re-debugged. Same formula — ceiling of duration plus 0.4, times 30 — re-calculated, re-billed. Every run. That's the rewrite tax. You pay it in tokens and you pay it in drift.

Here's the axis. Some work must come out exact — two runs must agree. Some work may vary — the second run can be different, even better. Scripts own the exact. Prompts own the variable. That's not preference. It's a decision rule.

In this pipeline, one belt of scripts handles every reel and every brand. generate_audio.py, compile.py, remotion_scenes.py — not one per channel, one for all of them. The skill says what. The script does exactly. And there is only one script to debug.

The honest self-own: generate_audio.py exists twice in this repo. The fork happened when a new brand needed tweaks and someone copied instead of parameterized. Two scripts means the bug lives in both. One fix lands in one. The other runs wrong for weeks.

The law: reuse before you write. If a script exists that does the thing — call it. If it almost does the thing — parameterize it. Write new code only when neither works. Writing is the last resort, not the first move.

Script or prompt — one page. Two runs must agree: script. Two runs may differ: prompt. Pay the rewrite tax once to write it, never again to call it. One belt, shared across all brands. Reuse before you write. Parameterize before you copy. Write only when neither works.

artifactLines: Two runs must agree → script. Two runs may differ → prompt.
The rewrite tax: re-deriving exact logic costs tokens and drift every run.
One belt: one script per operation, shared across all brands and reels.
Forks breed bugs: copy instead of parameterize and you own two broken scripts.
Reuse before you write. Parameterize before you copy. Write only when neither works.
